meanings.definition: Dissatisfaction with one's circumstances; lack of contentment; a person who is dissatisfied.
meanings.example: The discontent among middle management had been growing for years, fuelled by the perception that their concerns were systematically ignored in strategic decisions.

meanings.definition: The quality of behaving or speaking in such a way as to avoid cause offence; the freedom to decide what should be done in a particular situation.
meanings.example: The panel was given wide discretion in determining what evidence to admit, a feature of the process that was both its strength and the source of subsequent challenge.

meanings.definition: To recognise a distinction; to make an unjust distinction in the treatment of people on grounds of race, sex, age, etc; to distinguish between.
meanings.example: The policy discriminated against part-time workers in a way that the tribunal found could not be objectively justified.

meanings.definition: To release or detach from something; to withdraw from an activity; to stop being involved.
meanings.example: The chair decided to disengage from the process after it became clear that the ground rules had been agreed without her input.

meanings.definition: Too large or too small in comparison with something else; not proportional.
meanings.example: The sanction was widely criticised as disproportionate, far exceeding what the breach warranted when measured against any comparable case.

meanings.definition: To interrupt the normal course or unity of; to cause disorder.
meanings.example: The announcement disrupted the carefully managed sequence of events the team had planned to maintain narrative control throughout the launch.

meanings.definition: The expression of opposition or disagreement; holding or expressing opinions at variance with those commonly held.
meanings.example: Two members of the committee filed a note of dissent, setting out why they could not accept the majority's conclusion on the core question.

meanings.definition: Seeking to impose a doctrine in all circumstances without regard to practical considerations; dogmatic.
meanings.example: The approach was criticised as doctrinaire, applying an ideological template regardless of the specific conditions that varied considerably between sectors.

meanings.definition: Able to withstand wear, pressure, or damage; hard-wearing; lasting.
meanings.example: The settlement was not durable, collapsing within two years under the pressure of exactly the circumstances the dissenting committee members had predicted.

meanings.definition: Involving many carefully arranged parts; detailed and complicated; to develop or present in detail.
meanings.example: The elaborate structure of sub-committees and working groups that had been put in place turned out to be an obstacle rather than an enabler of effective decision-making.

meanings.definition: To give someone the authority or power to do something; to make someone stronger and more confident.
meanings.example: The policy was designed to empower front-line staff to make operational decisions without waiting for approval from a management layer that had become a bottleneck.

meanings.definition: To contemplate or conceive of as a possibility; to form a mental picture of.
meanings.example: The original design envisaged a phased rollout, but the pressure to show early results led to a simultaneous launch that overwhelmed capacity.

meanings.definition: Fair and impartial; relating to equity as a branch of law; just.
meanings.example: A more equitable distribution of the proceeds was sought by the smaller members, who argued that the existing formula had been designed when circumstances were very different.

meanings.definition: To escape or avoid by cleverness or deception; to avoid dealing with something honestly.
meanings.example: The question was evaded in the initial response, leading the interviewer to press again and accept a more specific answer.

meanings.definition: Serving as a desirable model; representing the best of its kind; serving as a warning to others.
meanings.example: The committee cited the organisation as an exemplary case of how a voluntary code of practice could achieve compliance without the need for formal regulation.

meanings.definition: To free someone from an obligation or liability; not subject to a rule or regulation.
meanings.example: Small charities were exempted from the most burdensome reporting requirements on the grounds that the compliance cost would be disproportionate to the regulatory benefit.

meanings.definition: Stated clearly and in detail; leaving nothing implied; not vague.
meanings.example: The agreement contained an explicit prohibition on the use of the data for secondary purposes, a provision that was later found to have been breached.

meanings.definition: To make an action or process easy or easier; to preside over or moderate.
meanings.example: The role of the secretariat was to facilitate the negotiations by managing the flow of information and ensuring that both sides had access to the same technical material.

meanings.definition: To lose strength or momentum; to speak hesitantly; to stumble or waver.
meanings.example: The programme began to falter when the funding that had been committed for the third phase was diverted to a more politically visible priority.

meanings.definition: Unlikely and unconvincing; implausible.
meanings.example: The explanation offered for the missing documents was described as farfetched by the investigator, who found it inconsistent with the established chronology of events.

meanings.definition: To display ostentatiously; to show off; to ignore a convention openly and unashamedly.
meanings.example: The spending on the refurbishment was described as flaunting resources at a time when core services were being reduced for lack of funds.

meanings.definition: To impose an unwelcome or unnecessary person or thing on someone; to pass off as genuine.
meanings.example: The amendment was seen as an attempt to foist a condition that had been rejected at an earlier stage onto the agreement through a side process.

meanings.definition: The outer or less important part of something; an unconventional or minority group; to border.
meanings.example: What had been a fringe position in academic debate five years earlier had become the orthodox view among practitioners by the time the report was published.

meanings.definition: Sparing or economical with regard to money or food; simple and costing little.
meanings.example: The new administration adopted a notably frugal approach to public expenditure, cancelling the refurbishment programme and moving several departments to smaller premises.

meanings.definition: To reproduce a message in a confused and distorted way; to scramble.
meanings.example: The message had been garbled in transmission, leading the recipient to take actions based on instructions that did not accurately reflect what had been sent.

meanings.definition: Lean and haggard, especially because of suffering, hunger, or age; grim or desolate in appearance.
meanings.example: The gaunt figure of the building, stripped of its cladding and awaiting demolition, had become an emblem of the failed regeneration strategy.

meanings.definition: Dark or poorly lit; causing or feeling depression or despondency; offering little hope.
meanings.example: The economic forecasts presented a gloomy picture, with most scenarios projecting a further contraction before conditions stabilised.

meanings.definition: Very severe or serious; causing pain or suffering; flagrant.
meanings.example: The tribunal characterised the failure as grievous, noting that it had persisted over years and had been brought to the attention of senior management on multiple occasions.

meanings.definition: Given or allowed only reluctantly; reluctant; unwilling.
meanings.example: The concession was made in a grudging manner that left the other party under no illusion that it would be honoured in spirit as well as letter.

meanings.definition: Easily persuaded to believe something; credulous; unsuspecting.
meanings.example: The scheme targeted the gullible through a combination of false authority and manufactured urgency, exploiting well-documented patterns in human decision-making under pressure.

meanings.definition: Unoriginal and trite; lacking freshness; overused.
meanings.example: The speech relied on hackneyed phrases that failed to persuade an audience that had heard the same arguments deployed in support of every preceding initiative.

meanings.definition: To be quick to do something; to cause something to happen sooner; to move with urgency.
meanings.example: The disclosure of the information hastened a departure that would in any event have become inevitable once the investigation reached its conclusion.

meanings.definition: To pay careful attention to; to take notice of advice or warning.
meanings.example: Had the organisation heeded the warnings issued in the first review, the more serious problems identified three years later would almost certainly have been avoided.

meanings.definition: Diverse in character or content; composed of different or dissimilar elements.
meanings.example: The committee drew on the experience of a heterogeneous group of practitioners, ensuring that the findings reflected a genuinely wide range of sectoral contexts.

meanings.definition: A system in which members of an organisation or society are ranked according to relative status or authority; a ranking.
meanings.example: The hierarchy within the organisation was so rigid that junior staff felt unable to pass information upwards without going through multiple intermediary levels.

meanings.definition: To make it difficult for someone or something to do or to progress; to obstruct.
meanings.example: The lack of data-sharing agreements between the relevant agencies hindered the investigation and made it impossible to establish the full picture in the time available.

meanings.definition: Having a hole or empty space inside; without real significance; sounding like a hollow space.
meanings.example: The pledge was ultimately hollow, containing no mechanism for enforcement and no consequence for the party that chose to disregard it.

meanings.definition: Of the same kind; alike; consisting of parts all of the same kind.
meanings.example: The board was criticised for being too homogeneous in its composition, lacking the diversity of background needed to challenge the dominant assumptions.

meanings.definition: Showing or feeling opposition or resistance; unfriendly; of or relating to a military enemy.
meanings.example: The hostile reception from industry stakeholders reflected a deeper disagreement about whether the proposed framework was appropriate for the nature and scale of the businesses it would cover.

meanings.definition: A person who pretends to have virtues or moral beliefs that they do not actually have.
meanings.example: He was labelled a hypocrite for advocating environmental measures in public while presiding over a company that was one of the largest polluters in the sector.

meanings.definition: The practice of claiming to have higher standards or more admirable beliefs than one actually possesses.
meanings.example: The hypocrisy at the heart of the position was exposed when documents emerged showing that the official line was contradicted by private assessments shared with advisers.

meanings.definition: Based on or relating to a system of ideas or ideals; relating to ideology.
meanings.example: The debate was as much ideological as technical, with the two sides offering different visions of the role that public institutions should play in the market.

meanings.definition: Not showing or feeling emotion; calm; unmoved.
meanings.example: Her impassive expression throughout the questioning gave no indication of whether she was troubled by the specific documents that were placed before her.

meanings.definition: To delay or prevent by obstructing; to hinder the progress of.
meanings.example: The absence of a shared data infrastructure impeded the ability of the agencies to coordinate their response in the early stages of the crisis.

meanings.definition: Not showing proper respect; irrelevant or inappropriate to the matter in question; bold.
meanings.example: The impertinent tone adopted in correspondence with the regulator was itself cited in the subsequent enforcement review as evidence of poor governance culture.

meanings.definition: Not seeming reasonable or probable; failing to convince.
meanings.example: The explanation that the files had been misfiled rather than destroyed was considered implausible in light of the systematic pattern identified by the audit team.

meanings.definition: To create or perform spontaneously without preparation; to make something from whatever is available.
meanings.example: The team was forced to improvise when the agreed support materials failed to arrive, drawing on their collective experience to fill the gap.

meanings.definition: Tending to stir up conflict; an incendiary device; relating to fire.
meanings.example: The speech was widely condemned as incendiary, deploying language that those present recognised as a deliberate provocation to an already inflamed situation.

meanings.definition: Having no particular interest or concern; neither good nor bad; uncaring.
meanings.example: The institution had been indifferent to the warnings over many years, treating repeated concerns as minority views rather than indicators of a systemic problem.

meanings.definition: Feeling or showing anger or annoyance at what is perceived as unfair treatment.
meanings.example: Staff were indignant at the suggestion that the error had been their responsibility, given that the guidance they had been following was demonstrably unclear.
